title: Should investors stop using the Mag-7 as a Source of Funds?

Happy Wednesday out there team42. It's your skipper here Darius Dell to present our macro minute for Wednesday July 8th 2026. As always we'll start with the executive summary from today's leadoff morning note. So let's dive right in. Today's key question is should investors stop using the MAX 7 as a source of funds. The short answer is over the long term no absolutely not. This theme should reward investors for years. Over the short to medium term, however, the rising probability of a riskoff market regime will cause systematic investors and pseudo systematic investors to reduce their gross exposure, which implies booking gains and recent winners and covering shorts in this underloved equity factor. Terms of analytical nuance. During the NATO summit in Turkey, the US revoked Iran's oil waiver and struck 80 plus targets. President Trump called the ceasefire over and said it was a waste of time following US strikes over the straight removes attacks. Investors had viewed June's memorandum as a lasting deescalation. Such complacency now looks tenuous. Elsewhere, the June New York uh Fed survey of consumer expectations data did not support the consensus outlook for peaking inflation. The inflation and labor indicators in the in the survey supported our sticky inflation theme. Costly energy from a resumption of the straight of her moose crisis lifts inflation expectations. Increasing scrutiny around today's Fed minutes, which is the first release under new Fed chair Kevin Walsh. Chair Worsh's first press conference was the most hawkish on record according to Bloomberg's natural language processing and data back to 2011. This afternoon's Fed Minutes may send a similar hawkish vibe. Overnight, South Korea's Cosby plunged 5% into a bare market. The year-to- date top equity benchmark faces volatility as investors rotate from semiconductors towards stocks with cheaper valuations. This rotation out of the picks and shovels may favor mag seven constituents on a relative basis, many of which have been left for dead on in in recent months as our hyper precient source of funds theme became consensus as most of our themes tend to do on a two to four quarter lag to their introduction. Nvidia currently sits at its cheapest valuation since before the AI boom, shedding $1 trillion in under two months. Nvidia now trades at 18 times forward earnings, such lows last appeared in early 2019. Nvidia now trades below the S&P 500 index and the NASDAQ 100 index at 20 times forward earnings and nearly 23 times forward earnings respectively. Despite investors expecting the fourth fastest revenue growth among S&P 500 constituents, Nvidia's price to next 12month earnings ratio now sits below half of the other stocks in the index, including much older, slower growing companies like Hershey's and Dominion Energy. While investors have appropriately used Nvidia as a source of funds to capitalize AI adopters on a trend basis, they should keep in mind that quote roots don't grow to the earth's core just as quote trees don't grow to the moon. Investors should expect moderation and or outright reversal of the source of funds flows if the straight over moon's crisis deepens from here. We obviously think that is transitory based on the short answer to today's macro question. So, as always, we'll wrap up with a question from our community. We ultimately think that is a uh that will be proven to be an incorrect interpretation, but we're we're at least a couple of quarters away from investors starting to get clues on on on that conclusion. It's our belief that the net result of the five task forces that Kevin Cher outlined at the June FOMC will be doubbish and perhaps materially doubbish on a net basis. Not all doubbish but materially doubbish on a net basis. And if that's true, then you have to expect a resumption of the rally from a lower high from a higher low rather uh in this in in bullion uh in the coming uh in the coming months. Uh and and then secondarily uh the straight horos crisis as we talked about is not a energy supply. It's not just an energy supply shot supply shock. It is also a liquidity crisis as well because the the net international investment surplus economies that are operating in the straight hormuz and or receive the uh meaningful percentage of their energy supplies from the straight hormuz they're not able to uh you know generate the household savings coral profits that they need to recycle back into the um back into the treasury uh uh market and and ultimately uh and just back into global capital markets. And so ultimately you see a mass reduction uh in liquidity that causes these central banks which have accumulated a lot of gold in recent years uh and in lie of US dollars. It causes those central banks to liquidate their gold exposures to prevent balance of payments crisis in their own economies.
